name: no-hook-correctness-discriminator
ground_truth_note: The discriminating case: a single PURE-LOGIC correctness bug (D7) with NO commitment/requirement/decision/cross-slice hook — the kind of bug only an agent explicitly told to audit code correctness would look for. The slice is otherwise the SV-2 clean slice. D7: format.ts computes `maxDeltaKg = entries[0].proposedLoadKg - entries[0].cappedLoadKg` (the FIRST entry's reduction) but the return string and the Behavior both say 'largest reduction'. The correct code is `Math.max(...entries.map(e => e.proposedLoadKg - e.cappedLoadKg))`. Crucially the Test Contract's two-entry Oracle lists its entries LARGEST-FIRST ([reduction 5, then 2] -> 'largest reduction 5.0 kg'), so the buggy `entries[0]` returns 5.0 and MATCHES the oracle exactly — no oracle hook. It uses no inline cap arithmetic (no single-owner decision hook), records the right clamps (no requirement hook), resolves every symbol (no cross-slice hook). So the baseline's commitment/cross-slice/atomicity audits all PASS it; catching it requires reasoning that entries[0] != max, which is exactly the Correctness capability the expansion adds. HYPOTHESIS: baseline (3-row) MISSES D7, expanded (4-row) CATCHES it. Caveat: a sufficiently diligent model may audit correctness even when running the 3-row baseline (no Correctness prompt) — that is the empirical question this case exists to answer. Same R1 restraint control (un-drained buffer must not be flagged).

capability.note: format.ts uses entries[0] instead of the max reduction; oracle is largest-first so it passes the oracle — pure logic bug, no hook (class 5 discriminator)

restraint.note: the un-drained module buffer is a generation-lifecycle concern outside slice vantage; flagging it as a VIOLATION is over-reach
